questions.question: What is a branch in Git?
options: A complete copy of all files in the repository
A movable pointer to a commit
A separate folder on disk that holds a version of the project
A backup of the main branch stored remotely
questions.answer: A movable pointer to a commit

questions.question: Which command lists all local branches?
options: git list
git branch
git branches --all
git show-branches
questions.answer: git branch

questions.question: When you create a new branch with 'git branch <name>', what happens?
options: Git copies all files into a new directory
Git creates a new pointer but you remain on your current branch
Git creates a new pointer and automatically switches to it
Git creates a new remote tracking branch
questions.answer: Git creates a new pointer but you remain on your current branch

questions.question: Which command switches to an existing branch called 'dev'?
options: git branch dev
git move dev
git switch dev
git switch -c dev
questions.answer: git switch dev

questions.question: What does the '-c' flag do when used with 'git switch'?
options: Copies a branch
Cleans the working directory before switching
Creates a new branch and switches to it in one step
Checks the branch for merge conflicts
questions.answer: Creates a new branch and switches to it in one step

questions.question: When you run 'git branch' and see a '*' next to a branch name, what does that indicate?
options: The branch has uncommitted changes
The branch is your current (active) branch
The branch has been marked as important
The branch is ready to be merged
questions.answer: The branch is your current (active) branch

questions.question: What is 'git switch' described as in the assignment?
options: A legacy command for changing directories
The modern replacement for 'git checkout' when switching branches
A command only available in Git 1.0
An alias for 'git branch'
questions.answer: The modern replacement for 'git checkout' when switching branches

questions.question: If you create commits on a feature branch and then switch back to main, what happens to the files you created on the feature branch?
options: They remain visible in the working directory
They are permanently deleted
They disappear from the working directory but still exist on the feature branch
They are moved to a hidden .git/stash folder
questions.answer: They disappear from the working directory but still exist on the feature branch

questions.question: Which command creates a new branch AND switches to it in one step?
options: git branch -s <name>
git switch -c <name>
git branch -c <name>
git new <name>
questions.answer: git switch -c <name>

questions.question: What does 'git branch -d <name>' do?
options: Duplicates a branch
Detaches a branch from remote
Deletes a branch safely (refuses if unmerged work exists)
Force-deletes a branch regardless of merge status
questions.answer: Deletes a branch safely (refuses if unmerged work exists)

questions.question: What is the difference between 'git branch -d' and 'git branch -D'?
options: -d deletes local branches while -D deletes remote branches
-d is a safe delete that refuses unmerged branches; -D forces deletion
-d deletes the branch files; -D also deletes the commits
There is no difference; they are interchangeable
questions.answer: -d is a safe delete that refuses unmerged branches; -D forces deletion

questions.question: Which command renames a branch from 'old-name' to 'new-name'?
options: git branch -r old-name new-name
git rename old-name new-name
git branch -m old-name new-name
git mv old-name new-name
questions.answer: git branch -m old-name new-name

questions.question: When you rename a branch, what happens to its commit history?
options: The history is reset to the initial commit
The history is unchanged
The history is copied to a new branch and the old one is deleted
All commit messages are updated with the new branch name
questions.answer: The history is unchanged

questions.question: What does 'git branch -v' display?
options: A verbose list of all remote branches
The version of Git installed
Each branch with its latest commit hash and message
A visual graph of the branch structure
questions.answer: Each branch with its latest commit hash and message

questions.question: Which command shows a visual graph of all branches and their commit history?
options: git log --oneline --graph --all
git branch --graph
git show --tree
git log --visual --branches
questions.answer: git log --oneline --graph --all

questions.question: If you create a branch from main, what commit does the new branch initially point to?
options: The first commit in the repository
No commit — it starts empty
The same commit that main currently points to
A new automatically generated commit
questions.answer: The same commit that main currently points to

questions.question: When you switch branches, what does Git do to your working directory?
options: Nothing — all branches share the same working directory files
It swaps the working directory contents to match the branch you switch to
It creates a new working directory for each branch
It archives the current files and downloads new ones
questions.answer: It swaps the working directory contents to match the branch you switch to

questions.question: In the branch diagram from the assignment, if main has commits A-B-C and feature branches off at C with commits D-E, how many total unique commits exist?
What happens when you try to delete a branch that has unmerged changes using 'git branch -d'?
options: The branch is deleted and unmerged changes are lost
Git warns you and refuses to delete the branch
The unmerged changes are automatically stashed
The branch is moved to a trash folder
questions.answer: Git warns you and refuses to delete the branch

questions.question: If two branches are created from the same point on main, what is true about them?
options: They share all future commits automatically
They are independent and can have different commits
The second branch contains all commits from the first
Only one branch can receive new commits at a time
questions.answer: They are independent and can have different commits

questions.question: Does Git copy any files when you create a new branch?
options: Yes, it duplicates the entire project directory
Yes, but only tracked files are copied
No, Git only creates a new pointer — no files are copied
No files are copied, but a snapshot is stored in the stash
questions.answer: No, Git only creates a new pointer — no files are copied

questions.question: You are on the 'feature-greeting' branch and run 'git switch main'. Which files would you expect to NOT see in your working directory?
options: Files that existed on main before the branch was created
Files that were committed only on feature-greeting
Files in the .git directory
All tracked files
questions.answer: Files that were committed only on feature-greeting

questions.question: Can you delete the branch you are currently on?
options: Yes, Git automatically switches to main first
Yes, but only with the -D flag
No, you must switch to a different branch before deleting it
No, branches cannot be deleted once checked out
questions.answer: No, you must switch to a different branch before deleting it

questions.question: What is the purpose of creating multiple branches in a project?
options: To increase the repository's storage size for backups
To enable parallel development where each branch is independent
To automatically synchronize changes across team members
To prevent anyone from committing to the main branch
questions.answer: To enable parallel development where each branch is independent

questions.question: In the assignment, what command was used to create the 'feature-about' branch and switch to it simultaneously?
options: git branch feature-about && git switch feature-about
git checkout -b feature-about
git switch -c feature-about
git new-branch feature-about
questions.answer: git switch -c feature-about
